summary: Jeff Atwood uses a game development war story about endless geometry patching to illustrate a common software development antipattern: continuously applying small fixes to fundamentally flawed code instead of addressing the root cause. The story describes a PS1 game port where floating-point to fixed-point conversion created countless geometry holes that testers found daily. Atwood confesses to falling into this same trap with Stack Overflow code, where fear of breaking working functionality led to months of incremental patches. He argues that the 'just this small change, just this once' rationalization is a dangerous lie developers tell themselves. The post advocates for overcoming fear and doing the fundamental rewrite when patching becomes a pattern.
key_points: The 'patch it again' cycle often stems from fear of rewriting working code, even when that code has become fundamentally flawed
There's never time to do it right, but there's always time to do it over - a costly paradox in software development
Working code that requires constant tweaking is a sign of underlying architectural problems that patches cannot solve
Self-awareness about the 'just this small change' rationalization is the first step toward breaking the patching cycle
Fear of breaking core functionality often leads to worse long-term outcomes than a proper rewrite would
money_quotes: There's never time to do it right, but there's always time to do it over.
It's the kind of code that, when submitted for code review, you're tempted to self-deprecatingly introduce as have you met my dog, patches?
The problem here was that the geometry was bad.
It was obvious this was working code which had become crufty over time, but it was working.
It's easy to mentally justify 'just this small change, just this once' rather than the fundamental rewrite you really need.
When you finally realize you've spent the last six months telling yourself the same 'just this small change, just this once' lie, then you've met your dog patches, too.
key_insight: When you find yourself repeatedly patching the same code with 'just this small change,' you're not fixing the problem—you're postponing the inevitable rewrite while accumulating technical debt.
